1: beyom kallot Mosheh lehaqim et hamishkan: 'On the day Moses finished (kallot, completing/finishing — from kalah) setting up (lehaqim, to cause to stand) the tabernacle.' The temporal marker links this offering ceremony directly to the completion and anointing of the sanctuary narrated in Exod 40 and Lev 8. The verb mashach (anointed) appears twice in this verse — both the structure and its furnishings receive anointing, constituting the inauguration of the sacred space. This is the longest chapter in the Torah precisely because the tribal leaders' response to the completed tabernacle is given in full, tribe by tribe, as a testament that the entire nation participates equally in its dedication.
2: hanosim: 'The leaders' — literally those who 'stand over/supervise,' from nasa' (to lift up, to carry). The same root generates nasi (prince/leader), a term used consistently in Numbers for these twelve tribal heads. They are further identified as piqudei hamifqadim (those over the musters, those who presided at the census) — connecting them to the leaders named in Numbers 1:5-15 who assisted Moses in the census. The leaders who were charged with numbering the people now lead the dedication offering, making this a capstone event of Israel's military and cultic organization.
3: shesh agalot tsav ushney asar bakar: Six covered wagons (agalot tsav) and twelve oxen. The term tsav (or tsa') is rare — possibly denoting a covered or roofed cart (LXX: hamaxas lampēnikous, 'covered wagons'). The ratio is one cart per two leaders and one ox per leader. Twelve leaders, twelve oxen, six carts — the arithmetic of communal equality. The carts are needed for Levitical transport of the tabernacle's dismantled components; Num 4 specified the carrying duties of each Levitical clan, and this provision addresses the heaviest and bulkiest items.
4: vayedaber YHWH el Mosheh lemor: The standard speech-introduction formula. YHWH's acceptance of the leaders' gift is not taken for granted — the text marks it as a deliberate divine response. The leaders' initiative is appropriate and will be welcomed, but Moses waits for YHWH's word before distributing.
5: qach mehem vehayu la'avod et avodaת ohel mo'ed: 'Accept from them for the service of the tent of meeting.' The idiom qach mehem (take from them) is used when YHWH receives a voluntary offering; the carts and oxen become dedicated cultic property. The distribution principle — lekol ish kephi avodato (to each man according to his service) — establishes proportionality: Levitical clans receive resources proportional to their transport burden.
6: vayiqach Mosheh et haagalot ve'et habakar: Moses executes YHWH's command immediately. The verse is a simple narrative report — compliance with the divine word is noted without elaboration, the standard pattern of Mosaic obedience in Numbers.
7: shetayim agalot ve'arba'ah vakar natan livnei Gershon: Two carts and four oxen to the Gershonites — the lighter portion, befitting their charge (the soft goods: curtains, coverings, screens). The Gershonites' items are bulky but manageable; two carts suffice for the tent coverings and courtyard hangings. Their allotment is given kephi avodatam (according to their service), reinforcing proportional allocation.
8: ve'arba' agalot ushmonat bakar natan livnei Merari: Four carts and eight oxen to the Merarites — double the Gershonite share. The Merarites carry the structural elements: the tabernacle's frames (qerashim), bars (berihim), pillars, and sockets. These are far heavier than the Gershonite soft goods. The service is al yad Ithamar (under the hand of Ithamar) — the younger son of Aaron oversees the Merarite transport logistics, while Eleazar oversees Kohath.
9: ulivnei Qohat lo natan ki avodaת haqqodesh aleihem bakkattef yissa'u: 'But to the sons of Kohath he gave nothing, because the service of the holy things was upon them — on the shoulder they shall carry.' The Kohathites carry the most sacred objects (ark, table, lampstand, altars) and must bear them on their shoulders, not on carts. This prohibition is deadly serious: when David later transports the ark by cart rather than by Levitical shoulder-carrying, Uzzah dies touching it to steady it (2 Samuel 6:6-7). The rationale of this verse makes Uzzah's death intelligible: the cart violates the shoulder-carry requirement established here. The qodesh (holy things/sanctuary) requires direct human contact — mediated through the pole-and-ring system — not mechanized transport.
10: vayaqrivu hanesim et chanukkat hamizbe'ach beyom himasheach oto: 'The leaders brought the dedication (chanukkah) offering for the altar on the day it was anointed.' The word chanukkah appears here in one of its earliest usages — the root chanak means to dedicate/initiate, to train up (Proverbs 22:6). This is the inauguration of the altar's liturgical use; the Maccabean festival later called Hanukkah (165 BCE rededication of the temple) derives its name from this same root and echoes this dedication ceremony. The leaders' offering specifically dedicates the altar, not merely the tabernacle.
11: nasi echad leyom nasi echad leyom yaqrivu et qorbanam: 'One leader per day, one leader per day, shall bring his offering.' The deliberate repetition (nasi echad leyom twice) is emphatic — no two leaders bring offerings simultaneously. YHWH's instruction ensures that each tribe's dedication is individual and complete, not diluted into a corporate ceremony. The 12-day sequence gives each tribe equal liturgical standing, with equal gifts, equal recognition. The word qorban (offering/gift) is from qarav (to draw near, to approach); the offering is the vehicle of approach to YHWH.
12: vayehi hamaqriv bayom harishon et qorbano Nachshon ben Amminadav lemateh Yehudah: Judah goes first — consistent with Judah's precedence in the census list (Numbers 1:7, Numbers 2:3) and the marching order (Numbers 2:9). Nahshon son of Amminadab is identified in 1 Chronicles 2:11 as an ancestor of David (and in Matthew 1:4, of Jesus). His name Nachshon is of uncertain meaning (possibly connected to nachash, serpent, or a verb form); his father's name Amminadab means 'my people is generous/noble.' The Judahite precedence established here will reappear throughout the wilderness narrative and ultimately in the tribal blessings pointing toward David and the Messiah.
13: qorbano qe'arat kesef achat shloshah umei'ah mishqalah mizraq echad kesef shiv'im shaqel beseqel haqqodesh: The offering vessels: one silver dish (qe'arah, a flat plate or platter, root uncertain — possibly related to qara' to scoop out) weighing 130 shekels; one silver basin (mizraq, from zaraq, to toss/scatter — the vessel used for dashing/tossing sacrificial blood against the altar) weighing 70 shekels. Both are 'by the sanctuary shekel' (beseqel haqqodesh), the standardized cultic weight unit. Together they weigh 200 shekels (≈2.3 kg) — substantial silver dedicated to the sanctuary service. Both vessels are 'filled with fine flour mixed with oil' for the grain offering (minchah), indicating that the silver gift includes both vessel and contents.
14: kaph achat asarah zahav mele'ah qetoret: One gold pan (kaph, literally 'palm/hollow of the hand') weighing 10 shekels, full of incense (qetoret, from qatar, to cause smoke to rise). The kaph is named for its cupped-palm shape — a small incense spoon rather than a full vessel. Gold is appropriate for the most sacred item: the incense burned before YHWH. At 10 shekels per pan × 12 pans = 120 shekels of gold total (Numbers 7:86). The qetoret is the sacred incense compound prescribed in Exodus 30:34-38, burning only on the golden incense altar inside the tent.
15: par echad ben bakar 'ayil echad keves echad ben shnatow le'olah: For the burnt offering ('olah, ascent offering — the animal ascends entirely as smoke, nothing retained by the offerer): one young bull (par ben bakar, a bull literally 'a son of the herd'), one ram ('ayil), one male lamb of the first year (keves ben shnato). The triad bull + ram + lamb covers the range of value and size in sacrificial animals, expressing comprehensive devotion. The 'olah represents total dedication — nothing withheld.
16: se'ir izzim echad lechattat: One male goat (se'ir izzim, literally 'a hairy goat') for a sin offering (lechattat). The chattat (sin/purification offering) is included in each tribal dedication offering — even the dedication of the altar involves acknowledgment of the people's need for atonement and purification. No approach to YHWH is presupposed as righteous in itself; the sin offering accompanies even the most joyous cultic moments.
17: ulizevach hashlamim shenei vakar chamishah 'eilim chamishah sattudim chamishah kevasim benei shanah zeh qorban Nachshon ben Amminadav: The fellowship offering (shelamim, from shalom — well-being, wholeness, completion): two bulls, five rams, five male goats (sattudim, he-goats), five yearling lambs. The shelamim is the communal meal offering: portions go to YHWH (fat/blood), the priest, and the offerer's household — it is the only sacrifice where the offerer eats. The closing formula — 'This was the offering of Nahshon son of Amminadab' — is repeated verbatim for all 12 leaders (Numbers 7:23, 29, 35, 41, 47, 53, 59, 65, 71, 77, 83), a literary device that gives each offering its own complete, named identity.
18: bayom hasheni hiqriv Nethanel ben Tsu'ar nasi Yissachar: Day 2 — Nethaneel son of Zuar, leader of Issachar. Nethanel means 'God has given' (natan + El); Tsu'ar means 'smallness/young.' Issachar was a tribe associated with agricultural labor (Genesis 49:14-15, 'a strong donkey lying between sheepfolds') and scholarly wisdom (1 Chronicles 12:32, 'men who had understanding of the times').
19: The offering is identical in every detail to the Day 1 offering (Numbers 7:13-17): silver dish (130 shekels), silver basin (70 shekels), both filled with fine flour and oil for a grain offering. The identical offerings underscore the equal standing of all 12 tribes before YHWH — no tribe receives preference in the dedication ceremony.
20: Gold incense pan weighing 10 shekels, full of incense — identical to Numbers 7:14 for Judah. The identical incense offerings across all 12 leaders signal that all Israel's worship is uniform, entering by the same means and with the same fragrant prayer (qetoret as a metaphor for intercession, cf. Psalms 141:2).
21: Burnt offering identical to Numbers 7:15: one young bull, one ram, one yearling lamb. Total dedication expressed by all twelve tribes through the same offering — the uniformity of the 'olah across all tribes represents the uniformity of Israel's consecration to YHWH.
22: One male goat for a sin offering — identical to Numbers 7:16. The sin offering's presence in every tribal dedication reminds the reader that even Issachar's joyful gift requires the acknowledgment of sin and the need for atonement.
23: Fellowship offering (two bulls, five rams, five male goats, five yearling lambs) and closing formula: 'This was the offering of Nethaneel son of Zuar.' The closing formula individualizes and memorializes each offering, ensuring that the liturgical record preserves each tribe's name.
24: bayom hashlishi Eli'av ben Chelon nasi livnei Zevulun: Day 3 — Eliab son of Helon, leader of Zebulun. Eli'av means 'My God is father'; Chelon means 'strength.' Zebulun, the sixth son of Leah (Genesis 30:20), was associated with maritime activity (Genesis 49:13) and is notable in Judges 5 for bravery. The tribe camps adjacent to Issachar in the Levitical arrangement (Numbers 2:7).
25: Silver dish (130 shekels) and silver basin (70 shekels) filled with fine flour and oil — the grain offering component identical to all other tribe offerings. See Numbers 7:13 for full lexical commentary on the vessel terminology.
26: Gold incense pan (10 shekels) full of incense — identical to Numbers 7:14. See that verse for the etymology of kaph and qetoret.
27: Burnt offering — one bull, one ram, one yearling lamb. Identical to Numbers 7:15. See that verse for commentary on the 'olah as a total-dedication offering.
28: One male goat for sin offering — identical to Numbers 7:16. See that verse for the role of the chattat in the dedication ceremony.
29: Fellowship offering (two bulls, five rams, five male goats, five yearling lambs) with closing formula: 'This was the offering of Eliab son of Helon.' The closing formula in Hebrew follows the fixed pattern zeh qorban X ben Y (this is the offering of X son of Y), preserving each leader's patronymic.
30: bayom harvi'i Elitsur ben Shedeur nasi livnei Re'uven: Day 4 — Elizur son of Shedeur, leader of Reuben. Elitsur means 'My God is rock'; Shedeur means 'Almighty is fire/light.' Reuben was Jacob's firstborn but lost his birthright (Genesis 49:3-4). In the wilderness arrangement, Reuben leads the second division (Numbers 2:10-16) but has already conceded the firstborn blessing to Judah — as reflected in Judah opening this sequence on Day 1.
31: Silver dish (130 shekels) and silver basin (70 shekels) — grain offering vessels identical to all other tribe offerings. The uniform weight specifications ensure that the sanctuary's silver vessels are of standardized weight for liturgical use.
32: Gold incense pan (10 shekels) of incense — identical to Numbers 7:14.
33: Burnt offering — bull, ram, yearling lamb — identical to Numbers 7:15.
34: One male goat for sin offering — identical to Numbers 7:16.
35: Fellowship offering with closing formula: 'This was the offering of Elizur son of Shedeur.' The repetition of this formula 12 times through the chapter is itself a form of liturgical honoring — each tribe is named and memorialized in the permanent record of the sanctuary's dedication.
36: bayom hachamishi Shelumi'el ben Tsurishaddai nasi livnei Shim'on: Day 5 — Shelumiel son of Zurishaddai, leader of Simeon. Shelumi'el means 'God is my peace/well-being' (from shalom + El); Tsurishaddai means 'My rock is the Almighty.' Simeon, second son of Jacob and Leah, will later be associated with the Baal Peor apostasy (Num 25 — the offender Zimri is identified as a Simeonite, Numbers 25:14), making this Day 5 dedication offering a poignant contrast to the later disgrace.
37: Silver dish and silver basin — grain offering vessels identical to all other offerings. See Numbers 7:13 for full commentary.
38: Gold incense pan — identical to Numbers 7:14.
39: Burnt offering (bull, ram, yearling lamb) — identical to Numbers 7:15.
40: One male goat for sin offering — identical to Numbers 7:16.
41: Fellowship offering with closing formula: 'This was the offering of Shelumiel son of Zurishaddai.'
42: bayom hashishi Elyasaph ben De'u'el nasi livnei Gad: Day 6 — Eliasaph son of Deuel, leader of Gad. Elyasaph means 'God has added'; De'u'el (or Reuel in some texts, Numbers 2:14) means 'friend of God.' Gad, a son of Zilpah, was associated with warrior qualities (Genesis 49:19: 'Gad shall be raided by raiders, but he shall raid at their heels'). The Gadites later choose territory east of the Jordan (Num 32), but here they join the unified dedication of the sanctuary.
43: Silver dish (130 shekels) and silver basin (70 shekels) — grain offering vessels. Identical to all other tribe offerings; see Numbers 7:13.
44: Gold incense pan (10 shekels) — identical to Numbers 7:14.
45: Burnt offering (bull, ram, yearling lamb) — identical to Numbers 7:15.
46: One male goat for sin offering — identical to Numbers 7:16.
47: Fellowship offering with closing formula: 'This was the offering of Eliasaph son of Deuel.'
48: bayom hashvi'i Elishama ben Ammihud nasi livnei Efrayim: Day 7 — Elishama son of Ammihud, leader of Ephraim. Elishama means 'My God has heard'; Ammihud means 'my kinsman is glory/splendor.' Ephraim, Joseph's younger son, received Jacob's primary blessing (Genesis 48:19-20). By the wilderness period, Ephraim leads the third tribal division (Numbers 2:18-24). The Ephraimite Joshua will succeed Moses — but here in Numbers 7, all distinctions of prominence are leveled by the identical offering.
49: Silver dish and silver basin — grain offering vessels. Identical to all other tribe offerings; see Numbers 7:13.
50: Gold incense pan — identical to Numbers 7:14.
52: One male goat for sin offering — identical to Numbers 7:16.
53: Fellowship offering with closing formula: 'This was the offering of Elishama son of Ammihud.'
54: bayom hashmini Gamliel ben Pedahtsur nasi livnei Menasheh: Day 8 — Gamaliel son of Pedahzur, leader of Manasseh. Gamliel means 'God has rewarded/requited'; Pedahtsur means 'the Rock has redeemed.' Manasseh, the older son of Joseph, received Jacob's secondary blessing — yet in Numbers the two Joseph tribes (Ephraim and Manasseh) are listed separately, making 13 tribal units, with Levi set apart, to maintain the 12-tribe count. Manasseh's Day 8 offering follows Ephraim on Day 7 — the younger before the older, as Jacob crossed his hands.
55: Silver dish and silver basin — grain offering vessels. See Numbers 7:13.
56: Gold incense pan — identical to Numbers 7:14.
57: Burnt offering (bull, ram, yearling lamb) — identical to Numbers 7:15.
58: One male goat for sin offering — identical to Numbers 7:16.
59: Fellowship offering with closing formula: 'This was the offering of Gamaliel son of Pedahzur.'
60: bayom hatesha'i Avidan ben Gid'oni nasi livnei Binyamin: Day 9 — Abidan son of Gideoni, leader of Benjamin. Avidan means 'My father is judge'; Gide'oni means 'tree-cutter/warrior.' Benjamin, Jacob's youngest and Rachel's second son, has the tribal territory that includes Jerusalem (later). The Benjaminite tribe will produce Israel's first king (Saul) and, later, Paul the apostle (Philippians 3:5: 'of the tribe of Benjamin').
61: Silver dish and silver basin — grain offering vessels. See Numbers 7:13.
62: Gold incense pan — identical to Numbers 7:14.
63: Burnt offering (bull, ram, yearling lamb) — identical to Numbers 7:15.
64: One male goat for sin offering — identical to Numbers 7:16.
65: Fellowship offering with closing formula: 'This was the offering of Abidan son of Gideoni.'
66: bayom ha'asiri Achi'ezer ben Ammishaddai nasi livnei Dan: Day 10 — Ahiezer son of Ammishaddai, leader of Dan. Achi'ezer means 'My brother is help'; Ammishaddai means 'My kinsman is the Almighty.' Dan, son of Bilhah, was associated with judicial function (Genesis 49:16: 'Dan shall judge his people as one of the tribes of Israel') but also with serpentine deception (Genesis 49:17). Dan is notably absent from the 144,000 sealed in Rev 7 — a detail many interpreters connect to Dan's later idolatry (Judg 18).
67: Silver dish and silver basin — grain offering vessels. See Numbers 7:13.
68: Gold incense pan — identical to Numbers 7:14.
69: Burnt offering (bull, ram, yearling lamb) — identical to Numbers 7:15.
70: One male goat for sin offering — identical to Numbers 7:16.
71: Fellowship offering with closing formula: 'This was the offering of Ahiezer son of Ammishaddai.'
72: bayom achad asar Pag'i'el ben Okran nasi livnei Asher: Day 11 — Pagiel son of Ocran, leader of Asher. Pag'i'el is unusual: it means 'God has met/encountered me' or 'event of God' (from paga', to encounter); Okran means 'trouble/affliction.' Asher, son of Zilpah, received the blessing of rich food and royal delicacies (Genesis 49:20). The prophetess Anna was from Asher (Luke 2:36), the only Asherite specifically identified in the NT.
73: Silver dish and silver basin — grain offering vessels. See Numbers 7:13.
74: Gold incense pan — identical to Numbers 7:14.
75: Burnt offering (bull, ram, yearling lamb) — identical to Numbers 7:15.
76: One male goat for sin offering — identical to Numbers 7:16.
77: Fellowship offering with closing formula: 'This was the offering of Pagiel son of Ocran.'
78: bayom shnem asar Achira ben Enan nasi livnei Naftali: Day 12 — Ahira son of Enan, leader of Naphtali. Achira means 'My brother is an enemy/evil' (unusual — possibly 'My brother is my shepherd' if ra'ah is the root); Enan means 'spring/eye.' Naphtali, son of Bilhah, was blessed with fleetness and beautiful words (Genesis 49:21: 'a deer let loose that bears beautiful fawns'). The tribal territory of Naphtali bordered Galilee — the region where much of Jesus's ministry would later occur, fulfilling Isaiah 9:1-2 (cited in Matthew 4:15-16).
79: Silver dish and silver basin — grain offering vessels. See Numbers 7:13.
80: Gold incense pan — identical to Numbers 7:14.
81: Burnt offering (bull, ram, yearling lamb) — identical to Numbers 7:15.
82: One male goat for sin offering — identical to Numbers 7:16.
83: Fellowship offering with closing formula: 'This was the offering of Ahira son of Enan.' The final tribal offering completes the 12-day dedication sequence. The formula is spoken for the twelfth time — twelve leaders, twelve days, twelve identical offerings, twelve named memories preserved in the cultic record.
84: zot chanukkah hamizbe'ach: 'This was the dedication (chanukkah) of the altar.' The summary formula uses the same root as Hanukkah. The dedicated items are enumerated: 12 silver dishes, 12 silver basins, 12 gold pans. The repeated numeral 12 — and the grand totals of silver (2,400 shekels), gold (120 shekels), and animals — expresses the completeness of all-Israel's participation. No tribe is missing; no gift is unequal. The altar's dedication is an act of the whole nation.
85: me'atah umei'ah hakkeseph vamizhiq shiv'im kol keshef hakkelim alpayim ve'arba' me'ot beseqel haqqodesh: The silver arithmetic: 12 dishes × 130 shekels = 1,560 + 12 basins × 70 shekels = 840 → total 2,400 shekels. The number 2,400 is notable as a multiple of 12 × 200, reflecting systematic equality. The insistence on recording exact weights 'by the sanctuary shekel' (beseqel haqqodesh) underscores the precision and verifiability of the sacred accounting.
86: kappot zahav shtem esreh mele'ot qetoret asarah asarah hakaph beseqel haqqodesh kol zahav hakappot esrim ume'ah: Twelve gold pans × 10 shekels each = 120 shekels of gold. The precision of the total (120 = 10 × 12) reflects the careful mathematics of equal tribal contributions. The incense contained in each pan was the sacred compound of Exodus 30:34-38 (stacte, onycha, galbanum, frankincense) — dedicated exclusively to YHWH's worship.
87: kol habakar la'olah shenim asar parim: The burnt offering animals in full: 12 bulls, 12 rams, 12 yearling lambs (36 total) with their grain offerings, and 12 male goats for sin offerings. The comprehensive enumeration — each sacrificial category totaled separately — creates a liturgical accounting of the entire dedication. The 12-fold repetition of each animal category represents Israel's complete self-offering to YHWH at the altar's inauguration.
88: vekol bevar zevach hashlamim: The fellowship offering totals: 24 bulls, 60 rams, 60 male goats, 60 yearling lambs — all 'for the dedication of the altar after its anointing.' The fellowship totals are double the burnt offerings (24 vs. 12 bulls) and five-fold for rams/goats/lambs (60 vs. 12), reflecting the communal-meal aspect: the shelamim feeds many (the offerer's household and the priests), while the 'olah is entirely consumed. The grand total of all animals offered across 12 days represents the most concentrated sacrificial ceremony in the Torah narrative outside of the temple dedication under Solomon (1 Kings 8).
89: uvevo Mosheh el 'ohel mo'ed ledabber itto vayishma et haqqol midabber elav me'al hakkapporet asher al aron ha'edut mibbein shney hakkeruvim: 'When Moses went into the tent of meeting to speak with him, he heard the voice speaking (midabber) to him from above the atonement cover (kapporet) that was on the ark of the testimony, from between the two cherubim.' The Niphal participle midabber (speaking reflexively/to itself) is unusual — it may indicate an inward or self-generated speech rather than an outward proclamation. The verse reveals why the entire tabernacle was built: so that YHWH might speak from between the cherubim on the ark. The 89-verse chapter of identical tribal offerings climaxes in a single sentence of theophanic communion — all that elaborate sacrifice is preparation for this: the voice of YHWH meeting with his servant from the mercy seat. The kapporet (from kapar, to cover/atone) is both the atonement cover and the locus of divine speech; the place of forgiveness is also the place where heaven and earth meet in conversation.
